cataloging_subject.label: The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The two distinct anonymous Middle English prose romances grouped together by Friedrich Brie's 1913 article and the CME00076 encoding. The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye have separate titles, narrative subjects, Lydgate source works, printed page runs, and manuscript loci, although Brie argues that one anonymous adapter composed both and the Bodleian records one hand copying them consecutively. This cataloged composite is distinct from John Lydgate's source poems, Bodleian MS. Rawl. D. 82 as a complete physical object, Fritz Jung's working transcript, Brie's German source study and edition, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in repository XML.

catalog_summary.editor: Friedrich Brie; manuscript transcript by Fritz Jung
catalog_summary.date: After 1422 and probably by the mid-fifteenth century; source edition published in 1913
catalog_summary.region: England; the surviving copy's language is localized to Northamptonshire, not necessarily the place of composition

titles.value: The Sege of Thebes and The Sege of Troye
titles.supplied_by: Combined from the two manuscript rubrics and modernized only by joining them with 'and'
notes: This whole-composite discovery title does not assert that the manuscript presents the two works under one collective medieval heading.

titles.value: Zwei mittelenglische Prosaromane: The Sege of Thebes und The Sege of Troy

notes: The current Bodleian catalog transcribes the opening rubric 'Here bigynnethe Sege of Thebes'; the XML and Brie print 'Here bigynneth þe Sege of Thebes.'

titles.value: In the tyme of the fulle worthi Josue, as the story reherceth by þe writing of þe noble clerke Boas
the which myscheues iournay and sege was iiije yere bifore Remus and Remulus bigane the fundacione of Rome

notes: Brie's article title normalizes the final word as Troy; the manuscript rubric and preferred part title use Troye.

titles.value: As the noble and worthi clerke Guydo writethe
And alwey the ende of euery tresone and falsenes to sorowe and myschef at the last. Amen.

agents.name: Friedrich Brie
notes: 1880–1948. Editor of the 1913 source article and author of its comparative source study.

agents.name: Fritz Jung
notes: Brie credits Dr. Fritz Jung of Pforzheim with the manuscript transcript used for the edition; no fuller identity was securely resolved.

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: Author of both verse source works, not the anonymous prose adaptations cataloged here.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous adapter, probably one person for both works

scope.description: Prose abridgment and adaptation of two Lydgate verse works

assertion.notes: Brie infers common authorship from poetic style and recurring elaboration. One copying hand does not by itself prove one adapter.

assertion.notes: Brie's circa-1450 wording and the modern mid-fifteenth-century characterization are approximate. The normalized upper bound therefore uses the current catalog's broader fifteenth-century attestation rather than converting circa 1450 into an exact terminus.

date_statements.display: After Lydgate completed Troy Book in 1420 and probably by about the middle of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The whole-composite summary uses 1422 because both works cannot predate the later of their two Lydgate sources. The normalized upper bound uses the broad fifteenth-century attestation rather than treating circa 1450 as exact.

form_statements.description: Two continuous prose narratives. The removed or adjacent verse mentioned in XML revision history and the following Gower extract are outside this cataloging boundary.

genre_statements.label: Classical romance
notes: The works recast legendary Theban and Trojan histories as late-medieval prose narratives of rulers, warfare, counsel, betrayal, and civic destruction.

subject_statements.label: Thebes and the Seven against Thebes
Fratricide, civil war, and civic destruction

summaries.value: Two anonymous Middle English prose romances abridge John Lydgate's great verse narratives of Thebes and Troy. The first recounts Oedipus, the rival claims of Eteocles and Polynices, the Seven against Thebes, fratricidal war, and Theseus's final intervention. The second begins with Jason and the Golden Fleece, follows the causes and battles of the Trojan War, and ends with Troy's betrayal and destruction and a moral against treason. CME00076 presents Friedrich Brie's 1913 single-manuscript edition with its short German transcription headnote and selected Lydgate references, but omits his longer German introduction.

Thebes is founded under Amphioun's line. Oedipus unknowingly kills his father and marries his mother Jocasta; their sons Eteocles and Polynices agree to alternate the crown, but Eteocles refuses to yield. Polynices gathers the Argive princes, the brothers kill one another, and Theseus finally compels burial for the fallen and punishes the Theban tyrant Creon.

The Troy narrative opens with Jason's Golden Fleece expedition and the first Greek destruction of Troy, then follows Priam's rebuilding, Paris's abduction of Helen, the Greek expedition and siege, Hector's death, Trojan betrayal, the wooden horse, and the destruction of the city. Its closing emphasizes the ruin produced by treason and falseness.

extent.description: Two complete prose works represented by two DIV1 elements. The encoding has 23 printed-page breaks: Thebes on pp. 47–52 and 269–272, Troy on pp. 273–285. It records 45 manuscript-folio change milestones, 37 notes, and 758 italic expansion spans. The manuscript work locus is inferred fols. 1r–24v inside a 34-leaf codex with six later endleaves.

content_parts.label: The Sege of Thebes
content_parts.summary: Anonymous prose abridgment of Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, from Oedipus through the war of the Seven and Theseus's intervention.

content_parts.label: The Sege of Troye
content_parts.summary: Anonymous prose abridgment of Lydgate's Troy Book, from Jason's expedition through the final destruction of Troy.

related_works.title: The Siege of Thebes
notes: Applies to part:thebes; John Lydgate; Middle English; composed circa 1421–1422.

related_works.title: Troy Book
notes: Applies to part:troy; John Lydgate; Middle English; composed 1412–1420.

related_works.title: Historia destructionis Troiae
notes: Guido delle Colonne; Latin; 1287. Applies to part:troy as the ultimate major source behind Lydgate's Troy Book, not the demonstrated direct source of the prose Sege.

evidence.locator: IDG; TEXT/@LANG; BODY/DIV1[1–2]; HEAD, P, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, HI, CLOSER, and SIGNED
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifier, two-work boundary, titles, incipits, prose form, Middle English and German editorial language, contents and subjects, page and folio locators, editorial responsibility, and source-edition representation.

evidence.locator: whole XML tree; DIV1-scoped XPath counts and ordered PB/MILESTONE attributes
evidence.summary: Verifies two DIV1 work containers; Thebes with ten PB, nineteen folio milestones, 28 notes, and 270 italic spans; Troy with thirteen PB, 26 folio milestones, nine notes, and 488 italic spans; and the unnumbered PB corresponding to printed p. 269.

Primary source for preferred part titles, two-work composition, anonymous shared-adapter argument, 1422–c.1450 date, Lydgate source relations, manuscript loci, Jung's transcript, editor responsibility, narrative texts, and editorial practice.

Official evidence for exact shelfmark, titles and rubrics, incipits and explicits, Middle English, English origin, fifteenth-century date, inferred Thebes and Troy loci, one Part 1 hand, complete codex extent, and adjacent contents.

evidence.citation: Nathaniel E. Griffin, 'The Sege of Troye,' PMLA 22.1 (1907), 157–200
evidence.summary: Independent primary evidence for Troy title, anonymous prose form, fifteenth-century and Southern-dialect assessments, unique witness, narrative scope, part incipit and explicit, and the historically important French-intermediary source theory.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, The Siege of Thebes: Introduction
evidence.summary: Current scholarship for Lydgate's circa-1421–1422 source poem and the prose Thebes redaction in Rawl. D. 82, characterized as a mid-fifteenth-century adaptation emphasizing epic battle portions.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Troy Book: Selections, Introduction
evidence.summary: Current scholarship for Troy Book's 1412–1420 composition and its use soon afterward as the source for a prose Sege of Troy through the fall of the city.

evidence.citation: Late Medieval English Scribes, Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Rawlinson D.82, manuscript ID 415
evidence.summary: Supports a 1450–1500 witness date, two-booklet physical account, hand boundaries, modern pencil foliation report, and Northamptonshire localization under LALME LP 6640. These facts remain scoped to the surviving copy.

evidence.citation: Malcolm Hebron, 'The Prose Siege of Thebes, Military Manuals and the Conduct of Sieges in the Later Middle Ages,' in The Medieval Siege (Oxford, 1997), 9–33
evidence.summary: Treats the prose Thebes as a mid-fifteenth-century English narrative concerned with the legendary battle, siege conduct, military lessons, heroic command, and the causes and meanings of urban warfare.

open_questions.question: How securely can one anonymous adapter be assigned to both prose romances?
next_steps: Reassess Brie's stylistic argument against current linguistic and narratological scholarship.
Keep separate anonymous authorship records for each work even while retaining the supported shared-adapter hypothesis.
Do not treat one manuscript hand as proof of one author.

open_questions.question: Can either prose adaptation be dated or localized more narrowly than after its Lydgate source and around the middle of the fifteenth century?
next_steps: Review LALME LP 6640 and later scholarship on the prose works' language.
Separate authorial dialect from the Rawl. D. 82 copy's Northamptonshire localization.
Reconcile Brie's mid-century witness estimate, the current broad fifteenth-century catalog date, and LMES's 1450–1500 copy date.

open_questions.question: What precise evidence finally excludes or qualifies Griffin's proposed unidentified French intermediary for The Sege of Troye?
next_steps: Compare Griffin's Gallicism and Dares arguments with Brie's Lydgate parallels and later source studies.
Retain Lydgate's Troy Book as the confirmed source in discovery metadata while preserving Griffin's earlier theory as historiography rather than an upstream entity.

notes: The German editorial headnote and source-edition publication date are documented but do not make the medieval prose works German or composed in 1913.
The two prose works appear to survive continuously only in MS. Rawl. D. 82. This record does not turn Lydgate manuscripts into witnesses of the distinct prose adaptations.
Brie's claim to print previously unknown texts conflicts with Griffin's 1907 Troy edition; the conflict affects publication history, not the immediate source of CME00076.
The XML's manuscript folio milestones begin after opening recto text and mark transitions, so their count is not a count of all photographed or extant page surfaces.

A complete physical-source facsimile must include all of MS. Rawl. D. 82 rather than only fols. 1r–24v; the work mappings remain Thebes 1r–10v and Troy 11r–24v, both currently described by the Bodleian as inferred loci.
